passages.explanation: As they approach Jerusalem, near Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sends two disciples: go to the village ahead. As you enter it, you'll find a colt tied there which no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it here. If anyone asks why, say: the Lord needs it and will send it back shortly. They find the colt and bring it to Jesus, throw their cloaks on it, and he sits on it. Many people spread their cloaks on the road, others spread branches they've cut from the fields. Those ahead and behind shout: Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David! Hosanna in the highest heaven! Jesus enters Jerusalem and goes into the temple courts. He looks around at everything, but since it's already late, he goes to Bethany with the Twelve. Mark's triumphal entry is understated compared to the other Gospels. Jesus looks around the temple — almost like an inspection — and then leaves. He doesn't cleanse it yet. He'll come back tomorrow. The colt that no one has ridden signals something new: a king is entering his city, but on terms no one expects.

The next day as they leave Bethany, Jesus is hungry. Seeing in the distance a fig tree in leaf, he goes to find out if it has any fruit. When he reaches it, he finds nothing but leaves, because it isn't the season for figs. Then he says to the tree: may no one ever eat fruit from you again. His disciples hear him say it. This is puzzling on the surface — it's not fig season, so why curse the tree? The fig tree is a symbolic act, not a frustration. In the Old Testament the fig tree regularly represents Israel (Jeremiah 8:13, Hosea 9:10). The tree has impressive leaves but no fruit. Mark sandwiches the temple clearing between the cursing and its result — the fig tree interprets the temple, and the temple interprets the fig tree.

On reaching Jerusalem, Jesus enters the temple courts and begins driving out those who are buying and selling there. He overturns the tables of the money changers and the benches of those selling doves. He won't allow anyone to carry merchandise through the temple courts. As he teaches them, he says: is it not written — my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations? But you have made it a den of robbers. The chief priests and the teachers of the law hear this and begin looking for a way to kill him, because they fear him — the whole crowd is amazed at his teaching. When evening comes, Jesus and his disciples go out of the city. The quote combines Isaiah 56:7 ('for all nations') and Jeremiah 7:11 ('den of robbers'). The temple was supposed to be a place where Gentiles could encounter God — the money-changing and selling happened in the Court of the Gentiles, blocking that purpose. Jesus isn't just angry at commerce; he's angry that Israel's worship has excluded the nations it was supposed to bless.

In the morning as they go along, they see the fig tree withered from the roots. Peter remembers: Rabbi, look! The fig tree you cursed has withered! Jesus replies: have faith in God. Truly I tell you, if anyone says to this mountain 'go, throw yourself into the sea' and doesn't doubt in their heart but believes that what they say will happen, it will be done for them. Therefore I tell you, whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours. And when you stand praying, if you hold anything against anyone, forgive them, so that your Father in heaven may forgive your sins. The withered fig tree is the conclusion of the symbolic action: religious Israel looks impressive (leaves) but produces nothing (no fruit) and is headed for judgment. Jesus uses the occasion to teach about faith and prayer — but the context matters. Faith isn't a technique for getting what you want; it's trust in the God who judges fruitlessness and demands authenticity.

They arrive in Jerusalem again. While Jesus is walking in the temple courts, the chief priests, teachers of the law, and elders confront him: by what authority are you doing these things? Who gave you authority to do this? Jesus replies: I will ask you one question. Answer me, and I'll tell you by what authority I do these things. John's baptism — was it from heaven, or of human origin? Tell me! They discuss it among themselves: if we say from heaven, he'll ask why we didn't believe John. But if we say of human origin — they fear the people, because everyone holds that John was truly a prophet. So they answer: we don't know. Jesus says: neither will I tell you by what authority I'm doing these things. The religious authorities try to trap Jesus with an authority question. He traps them instead. Their inability to answer honestly about John — a simpler question — disqualifies them from getting an answer about Jesus. If they can't handle the forerunner, they can't handle the one he announced.
